topic: A coach has to cut a player from the team
raw_output:
[story 1]

The air in the locker room felt thick, heavy with unspoken resentment. Coach Miller stood by the whiteboard, his chalk-dusted hands gripping the marker so tightly his knuckles were white. Across from him, Marcus slumped on the bench, his shoulders rounded, avoiding eye contact like a guilty animal. I watched him, a knot tightening in my stomach with every shallow breath I took. It wasn't anger that fueled me, not exactly; it was a profound sense of wrongness. He’d taken everything we built, every shared sacrifice, and treated it like cheap disposable material. When Miller finally spoke, his voice was low, measured, and utterly devoid of warmth. "Marcus, this isn't working. You're done." The sound of that finality echoed in the silence, and I felt a cold, hollow sensation spread through my chest, like ice water. I just stared at the floor, unable to summon any reaction, the silence roaring louder than any shout.

[story 2]

My jaw was clenched so hard I thought my teeth might crack. Watching Liam walk toward the exit felt like witnessing a slow-motion train wreck. He didn't look back; he just kept his gaze fixed on the scuffed toes of his sneakers, a picture of detached apathy. I wanted to lash out, to scream at him to explain what he’d done, but my throat felt constricted, dry and useless. Every muscle in my body was coiled tight, vibrating with a simmering, unpleasant energy. When the coach delivered the news, the words seemed to slide over me, meaningless static. I could feel a strange, nauseating lurch in my gut, a churning sensation that made my stomach clench. I managed a tight nod, a performance of acceptance that tasted like ash on my tongue. I just wanted the whole scene to vanish.

[story 3]

I kept running the scenario over and over in my head, trying to find a rational angle, but logic refused to settle. It was just… soiled. The way he’d played, the sheer lack of effort, the way he’d undermined every single person who had genuinely tried to lift him up. When the coach announced the decision, I noticed a subtle tremor running through my hands, a fine, involuntary shake. It wasn't fear; it was something much deeper, a profound sense of contamination. I looked at the coach, trying to gauge his reaction, but his expression was one of grim necessity, not malice. I felt physically ill, a wave of profound revulsion washing over me, making me want to scrub my skin raw just to feel clean again. I simply walked over to my gear bag, my movements stiff and deliberate, refusing to meet anyone's eyes.
